subject: Surveying
question_text: Which of the following scale is largest one?
solution:
Scale $1\text{ cm} = 50\text{ m}$

Option a. R.F. = $\frac{1}{5000}$
Option b. R.F. = $\frac{1}{42000}$
Option c. R.F. = $\frac{1}{300000}$
Option d. $1\text{ cm} = 50\text{ km}$ => R.F. = $\frac{1}{5000000}$

Since $\frac{1}{5000} > \frac{1}{42000} > \frac{1}{300000} > \frac{1}{5000000}$, Option (a) is the largest scale.

subject: Surveying
question_text: If the probable error in single observation is $\pm0.04\text{ m}$ and that of the mean is $\pm0.01\text{ m}$, then the number of observations is
solution:
$E_m = \text{probable error of the mean}$
$E_m = \frac{E_s}{\sqrt{n}}$
Where:
$E_s = \text{probable error of single observation}$,
$n = \text{no. Of observations}$.

$\sqrt{n} = \frac{E_s}{E_m}$
$n = \frac{0.04^2}{0.01^2}$
$n = 16$

subject: Surveying
question_text: A survey is conducted with a view to prepare the map of an area to a scale of $1:1000$. If a scale with least count of $0.1\text{ mm}$ is used for plotting, what would be the accuracy in length measurement in the field?
solution:
When the least count of a measurement is $1\text{ cm}$, we say the measurement is accurate till $1\text{ cm}$.
Here, Given the Scale being used is $1: 1000$.
It means that $1\text{ meter}$ would simply be a $1\text{ millimeter}$ on the map.
In the same way, we need to find a measurement which would be $0.1\text{ mm}$ on the map.
$1\text{ unit} \rightarrow 1000\text{ units}$
$0.1\text{ mm} \rightarrow x\text{ mm}$
By cross multiplying we get,
$x = 1000 \times 0.1\text{ mm} = 100\text{ mm} = 10\text{ cm} = 0.1\text{ m}$

solution:
Plane surveying - Plane surveying assumes the earth is flat. Curvature and spheroidal shape of the earth is neglected. In this type of surveying all triangles formed by joining survey lines are considered as plane triangles. It is employed for small survey works where errors due to the earth's shape are too small to matter.
Geodetic surveying - Geodetic surveying is another branch of surveying in which the curvature of the earth is considered when taking measurements on earth's surface. When survey extends over a large areas more than $250\text{ sq. km.}$ and degree of accuracy is also great.

subject: Surveying
question_text: The error due to bad ranging is
solution:
Errors in Chaining: - The errors that occur in chaining are classified as
- Compensating
- Cumulative.

Compensating Error: - The compensating errors are those which are liable to occur in either direction and hence tend to compensate i.e. they are not likely to make the apparent result too large or too small.

Cumulative Error: - The cumulative errors are those which occur in the same direction and tend to add up or accumulate i.e. either to make the apparent measurement always too long or too short.

Cumulative type of error in chaining:
- Incorrect length of tape.
- Tape not stretched horizontally.
- Error due to temperature.

subject: Surveying
question_text: The error due to eccentricity of inner and outer axes can be elimination by

options.a: reading both Vernier's and taking the mean of the two
options.b: talking both face observation and talking the mean of the two
options.d: taking mean of several readings distributed over different portions of the graduated circle

solution: Error due to eccentricity of Vernier's is primarily counteracted by reading both the Vernier's. if the zeros of the Vernier's A and B are not at the ends of the same diameter and exactly $180^\circ$ apart, the error occurs. In that case the difference between the two Vernier's readings will be constant but slightly different from $180^\circ$. The error can be eliminated by taking the mean of the two readings. If may be noted that if the Vernier is eccentric, no error is introduced in the angle so long as the same Vernier is used for the initial and final reading. It is clear from the above discussions that by taking the mean of the two Vernier readings, the error due to eccentricity of the centers and that due to eccentricity of Vernier's both are eliminated.

subject: Surveying
question_text: A star has a declination of $55^\circ\text{ N}$ . its upper culmination is in the zenith of the place. The altitude of the lower culmination is
solution:
when stars upper collimation is in zenith
polar distance = colatitude and declination = latitude
At lower culmination, zenith distance
$= 180 - (\text{latitude} + \text{declination})$
$= 180 - 2 \times 55$
$= 70^\circ$
Hence altitude $= 90^\circ - 70^\circ = 20^\circ$

subject: Surveying
question_text: A vertical photograph was taken from an aircraft flying at an altitude of 2000 m above mean sea level. The focal length of the camera is 175 mm. The scale of the photograph for a hill of an elevation of 250m is
solution:

$f = 175\text{ mm}$
$H = 2000\text{ m}$
$h_a = 250\text{ m}$
$= \frac{0.175}{2000-250}$
$= \frac{1}{10000}$
$\text{scale} = 1:10000$

subject: Surveying
question_text: When the wind effect is not considered during flight planning, the result is a
solution:
When the wind effect is not considered during flight planning, the result is a drift.
Drift is caused by the wind effect on an aircraft and is defined as the angle between the aircraft heading and the aircraft track. The direction in which an aircraft is pointed is called the heading. The actual path in which an aircraft travels over the ground is called the track.

subject: Surveying
question_text: An aircraft is flying at a ground speed of 200 km/h. The focal length of the camera is 20mm. The ground distance covered along the flight line between exposures is 2 km . The time between exposures is given by:
solution:
Given: Aircraft is flying at a ground speed of 200 km/h
focal length of the camera is 20mm
ground distance covered is 2 km
$\text{time between exposure} = \frac{\text{Distance}}{\text{Speed}}$
$\text{time between exposure} = \frac{2}{200}\text{ hr.}$
$= 36\text{ sec.}$

subject: Surveying
question_text:
The relief displacement is radial from
1) Isocentre on a vertical photograph
2) Principal point on a vertical photograph
3) Nadir point on a tilted photograph
4) Isocentre on a tilted photograph
Of the statements
solution:
The image of the tops of objects appearing in a photograph are displaced from the images of their bases. This distortion is known as relief displacement and causes any object standing above the terrain to lean away from the principal point of a photo radially.
Relief displacement is radial from:
- Principal point on a vertical photograph
- Nadir point on a tilted photograph

subject: Surveying

Where: f is the focal length
t is the angle of tilt.

subject: Surveying
question_text: Parallax bar is used to measure
solution:
A parallax bar measures Parallax difference.
The parallax bar is an instrument designed for use with a mirror stereoscope that has a stereo base of ten inches or less. It measures the relative position of identical points in both photographs, that is their parallax difference. The parallax difference can then be used to compare the relative heights.

question_text:
As compared to mirror stereoscope, lens stereoscope
1) causes less strain to the eyes of the user
2) is small in size
Of these statements
solution:
As compared to mirror stereoscope, lens stereoscope is small in size.
The pocket stereoscope, sometimes known as a lens stereoscope consists of two magnifying lenses mounted in a metal frame. Because of its simplicity and ease of carrying, it is the type used most frequently by military personnel.
The mirror stereoscope is larger, heavier, and more subject to damage than the pocket stereoscope. It consists of four mirrors mounted in a metal frame.

solution:
fathometer an instrument used in measuring the depth of water by the time required for a sound wave to travel from surface to bottom and for its echo to be returned.

subject: Surveying
question_text: An angle-measuring reading upto one-six of a degree on the main scale is equipped with a vernier having 19 main scale division divided into 20 parts. The correct least count for the instrument is
solution:
Least count = S - V.
Where S is the length of one division of main scale which is nothing but least count of main scale and V is the length of one division of vernier scale.
Therefore, $n \times V = (n-1) \times S$
Now, $S = \text{L.C of main scale} = (1/6)\text{ degrees} = (1/6) \times 3600\text{ sec} = 600\text{ seconds.}$
Now from $20 \times V = 19 \times 600$
$V = 570\text{ seconds.}$
Therefore Least Count $= S - V = 600 - 570 = 30\text{ seconds.}$

solution:

subject: Surveying
question_text: The standard time meridian in India is $82^\circ\ 30'\text{ E}$. If the standard time at any instant is $20\text{ hour } 10\text{ minutes}$, the local mean time for the place at a longitude of $20^\circ\text{ E}$ would be
solution:
Difference between standard meridian and the longitude of the given place = $82^\circ 30'\text{ E} - 20^\circ\text{ E} = 62^\circ 30'$
Local Mean time = ?
$\because$ every $1^\circ\text{ E}$ longitude increase in the 4 minutes time also increased.
$\Rightarrow$ standard meridian is $82^\circ 30'\text{ E} = 6\text{ hrs } 30\text{ min}$
$82^\circ 30'\text{ E} - 20^\circ\text{ E} = 62^\circ 30'$
$\Rightarrow 62^\circ 30' \times 4\text{ mins} = 250\text{ mins}$
Local mean time = Standard time - 250 minutes
$= 20\text{ hour } 10\text{ minutes} - 250\text{ min}$
$= 8:10 - 4:10$
$= 4\text{ PM}$

question_text: In an external focusing tacheometer, the fixed interval between stadia hairs is $5\text{ mm}$; the focal length of the objective is $25\text{ cm}$. and the distance of the vertical axis of the instrument from the optical centre of the objective is $15\text{ cm}$. Which one of the following is the set of constants of the tacheometer?
solution:
$C = \text{Additive constant} = (f+d)$
$K = \text{Multiply constant} = \left(\frac{f}{i}\right)$
Focal length $= 25\text{ cm}$
Distance from object glass to the trunnion axis is $15\text{ cm}$.
the fixed interval between stadia hairs is $5\text{ mm}$
$C = \text{Additive constant} = (f+d)$
$C = 15 + 25 = 40\text{ cm} = 0.4\text{ m}$
$K = \text{Multiply constant} = \left(\frac{f}{i}\right)$
$K = \frac{0.25}{0.005} = 50$

subject: Surveying
question_text:
The area of a plot is to be determined using Simpson's rule. The following offsets were taken to a boundary from points along a chain line, all measurements being in metres:
$12, 15, 22, 29, 36, 38, 31, 22, 17$.
These were taken at $100\text{ m}$ intervals. Consider the following steps in this regard:
1. $\frac{12+1}{2} = 19.5$
2. Sum of the odd ordinates $= 22 + 36 + 31 = 89$.
3. Sum of the even ordinates $= 15 + 29 + 38 + 22 = 104$
4. $12 + 17 = 29$
5. Area $= 100 (19.5 + 89 + 104)$
6. Area $= \frac{100}{3} (29 + 2 \times 89 + 4 \times 104)$
7. Area $= 100 \times 19.5 (104 - 89)$
The area of the plot can be determined by using the step listed above at
solution:
Simpson's rule: this rule is applicable only if ordinates are odd, i.e. even number of divisions.
If the number of ordinates are even, the area of last division maybe calculated separated and added to the result obtained by applying Simpson's rule to two remaining ordinates.
Even if first or last ordinate happens to be zero, they are not to be omitted from Simpson's rule.
Common distance, $d = 100\text{ m}$
Area $= d/3[(O_0+O_8) + 2(O_2+O_4+O_6) + 4(O_1+O_3+O_5+O_7)]$
$A = 100/3[(12+17) + 2(22+36+31) + 4(15+29+38+22)]$
Area $= \frac{100}{3} (29 + 2 \times 89 + 4 \times 104)$
Area $= 20766.67\text{ sqm}$

$X_B = 2000.00\text{ m}, Y_B = 1000.00\text{ m}$
The bearing of the line AB will be
solution:
We have the formula to find out the angle by use of coordinate.
Bearing of line AB = $\tan^{-1} (X_b - X_a / Y_b - Y_a)$
$= \tan^{-1} (2000-1000 / 1000-1000)$
$= \tan^{-1} (1000/0)$
$= \tan^{-1} (\text{infinity Or not define})$.
Now by use of trigonometric table find the angle which tan value should be infinity or not define
So the value is $90^\circ$.

The following bearings were taken in running a closed compass traverse ABCDA:

| Line | F.B. | B.B. |
|---|---|---|

| BC | 68°15' | 246°0' |
| CD | 310°30' | 135°15' |
| DA | 200°15' | 17°15' |

One would suspect local attraction at stations
solution: The occurrence of local attraction can be detected by observing the difference between the fore and back bearings. question_text:
In a closed traverse, the following computations were made:

| Line | Latitude (+) | Latitude (-) | Departure (+) | Departure (-) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| AB | | 73.0 | 440.5 | |
| BC | 535.0 | | 313.0 | |
| CD | 223.0 | | | 68.5 |

Consider the following conclusions drawn from the above computation:
1) Reduced bearing of DA = S 45° W
2) W.C.B. of DA = 225°
3) Departure of DA = +685.0m
4) Latitude of DA = +685.0m

Of these conclusions,
solution:
$$\Sigma L = 0 \implies -73.0 + 535.0 + 223.0 + L_{DA} = 0 \implies L_{DA} = -685.0\text{ m}$$
$$\Sigma D = 0 \implies 440.5 + 313.0 - 68.5 + D_{DA} = 0 \implies D_{DA} = -685.0\text{ m}$$
$$\tan \theta = \frac{-685.0}{-685.0} = 1 \implies \theta = 45^\circ$$
Reduced bearing of DA = S 45° W
WCB of DA = 270° - 45° = 225°

question_text: If the declination of a star is 70°10' N and the latitude of the observer is 49°50' N, then the values of the Zenith distance and altitude of the star at the lower culmination will be respectively.
solution:
At upper culmination,
Zenith Distance = $\delta - \theta$
And at Lower Culmination,
Zenith Distance = $180^\circ - (\delta + \theta)$
$= 180^\circ - (70^\circ 10' + 49^\circ 50')$
$= 180^\circ - 120^\circ = 60^\circ$

Altitude = $90^\circ - \text{zenith distance}$
$= 90^\circ - 60^\circ = 30^\circ$

subject: Surveying
question_text: In photogrammetric surveying, the image of the top of the hill is 90 mm from the principal point of the photograph. If the elevation of the top of the hill is 500 m and the flying height is 5000 m above datum, then the relief displacement is
solution:
Given:
the image of the top of the hill is 90 mm from the principal point
elevation of the top of the hill is 500 m
flying height is 5000 m
Relief displacement = $\frac{90 \times 500}{1000 \times 5000} = 0.009\text{ m} = 9\text{ mm}$

subject: Surveying
question_text:
Match List-I with List-II and select the correct answer using the codes given below the lists:
List-I (Elements of aerial Photographs)
A. perspective
B. plumb Point
C. Principal Point
D. Isocentre
List-II (Explanation of the elements)
1. The point where the perpendicular from the Optical centre of the Lens meets the photograph
2. The point where the rays from the objects converge
3. The point where the vertical through the optical centre meets the photograph
4. The point where the horizontal through optical centre meets the photograph
5. The point where the bisector of the angle between vertical & perpendicular from optical centre meet the photograph
solution:
Perspective: The point where the rays from the objects converge.
plumb Point: The point where the vertical through the optical centre meets the photograph
Principal Point: The point where the perpendicular from the Optical centre of the Lens meets the photograph
Isocentre: The point where the bisector of the angle between vertical & perpendicular from optical centre meet the photograph

question_text: A plane of an area drawn with the original scale of 1 cm = 10 m, has shrunk such that a line, originally 15 cm long on the plan, measures now 14.5 cm. the shrunk scale is given by 1 cm equal to
solution:

question_text: The length of a transition curve for a circular curve of radius 300 m and for a design speed of 15 m/s, when the rate of change of centrifugal acceleration is $0.3\text{ m/s}^3$, is
solution:

radius 300 m
design speed of 15 m/s
rate of change of centrifugal acceleration is $0.3\text{ m/s}^3$

subject: Surveying
question_text: If the line of sight between two station A and B, on sea, and 80 km apart, makes tangent at point A, then the minimum elevation of the signal required at B (considering the coefficient of refraction 'm' = 0.08 and the mean radius of earth as 6400 km) will be
solution:
Given:
Mean radius of earth = 6400 km
'm' = 0.08
D = 80 Km.
$h = \frac{D^2 \times (1 - 2m)}{2R} = \frac{80^2 \times (1 - 2 \times 0.08)}{2 \times 6400} = 0.42\text{ km} = 420\text{ m}$

subject: Surveying
question_text: In a closed theodolite traverse, the sum of the latitudes is +5.080 m and the sum of the departures is -51.406 m, sum of the traverse legs is 20.525 km. the accuracy of traverse is nearly equal to
solution:
Given:
$L = +5.080\text{ m}$
$D = -51.406\text{ m}$
Perimeter = 20525 m
Error/perimeter
Error = $\sqrt{L^2 + D^2} = 51.6564$
Perimeter = 20525
So, the answer is = $\frac{51.6564}{20525} \approx 1:400$

subject: Surveying
question_text: A sewer is laid from a manhole A to a manhole B, 250 m apart along a downward gradient of 1 in 125. If the reduced level of the invert at A is 205.75 m and the height of the boning rod is 3 m then, reduced level of the sight rail at B, is
solution:
Given:
- 250 m apart
- downward gradient of 1 in 125
- reduced level of the invert at A is 205.75 m
- height of the boning rod is 3 m
R.L. of invert at B = $205.75 - (250 \div 125) = 203.75\text{ m}$
RL of sight at B = $203.75 + 3 = 206.75\text{ m}$

subject: Surveying
question_text: If the standard meridian is $82^\circ 30'\text{E}$ and the standard time at longitude $90^\circ\text{E}$ is known to be 8 hr 30 m, the corresponding local mean time at the place will be
solution:
Difference between standard meridian and the longitude of the given place = $90^\circ - 82^\circ 30'\text{E} = 7^\circ 30'$
Local Mean time = ?
Every $1^\circ$ E longitude increase in the 4 minutes time also increased.
standard meridian is $82^\circ 30'"$ E = 8 hrs 30 min
$90^\circ - 82^\circ 30' = 7^\circ 30'$
$7^\circ 30' \times 4\text{ mins} = 30\text{ mins } 0\text{ seconds}$.
Local mean time = Standard time + 30 minutes 0 seconds = 8 hrs 30 min + 30 min 0 seconds. = 9hrs, 0 minutes and 0 seconds.

subject: Surveying
question_text: Given that Scale of photograph is 1cm = 100m, Size of photograph = 23 cm x 23 cm, Area to be converted = 150 sq.km, Longitudinal overlap = 60%, Side overlap = 30%. The total number of photographs required for covering the above area is
solution:
Given that:
Scale of photograph is 1 cm = 100m
Size of photograph = 23 cm $\times$ 23 cm
Area to be converted = 150 sq km
Longitudinal overlap = 60%
Side overlap = 30%
Covered area in one photograph = $23 \times 100\text{ m} \times 23 \times 100\text{ m} = 2300\text{ m} \times 2300\text{ m} = 2.3\text{ km} \times 2.3\text{ Km.}$
Length to covered = $\sqrt{150} = 12.247\text{ Km.}$
Number of strips required lengthwise: = $\frac{L}{(1 - P_L) \times \frac{l}{S}} = \frac{12.247}{(1 - 0.60) \times 2.3} = 13.31$
Number of strips required = $\frac{B}{(1 - P_s) \times \frac{b}{S}} = \frac{12.247}{(1 - 0.30) \times 2.3} = 7.6$
Required number of photographs = $13.31 \times 7.6 = 101$

subject: Surveying
question_text: During the measurement of a line by chain or tape in slopes, if the length of the line is 'l' and height difference between the ends of the line is 'h', then the correction to te measured length is more than $h^2/2l$ by
solution:
If h is the difference in level between end points separated by l, then the slope correction is $\frac{h^2}{2l} + \frac{h^4}{8l^3}$
It is here to note that the second term may be neglected if the value of h in a 20 m distance is less than 3m

subject: Surveying
question_text: In an old map, line PQ was drawn to a magnetic bearing of $6^\circ 32'$, the magnetic declination at that time begin $1^\circ$ East. The present magnetic declination is $9^\circ 42'$ East. The magnetic bearing to which the line is set at present is
solution:
TB = MB (+/-) declination.
So, T.B. = $6^\circ 32' + 1^\circ = 7^\circ 32'$
Now, M.B. = $7^\circ 32' - 9^\circ 42' = -2^\circ 10'$
So, $= (360^\circ - 2^\circ 10') = 357^\circ 50'$

subject: Surveying
question_text: In trigonometric levelling, if the horizontal distance between the instrument and the object is 3088 m, the coefficient of refraction is 0.07 and R sin 1" = 30.88 m, then the refraction correction in angular measure would be
solution:
Given:
$D = 3088$ m
coefficient of refraction, $m = 0.07$
Central angle, $\alpha = \frac{D}{R \sin 1"}$
$= 3088/30.88$
$= 100"$
Refraction correction = $m \times \alpha$
$= 0.07 \times 100$
$= 7"$ (-)

subject: Surveying
question_text:
In the given formula formats, L is the length of a base line into 'n' equal segments each of length 'd'. $O_1, O_2, ..., O_{n+1}$ are the ordinates at the sequential ends of the segments and $M_1, M_2, ..., M_3$ are the mid-ordinates of successive segments. Which of the following pairs of rules and the formulae for computation of the are standing on the base line are correctly matched?
1. Mid-ordinate Rule.............
$A = \left[ \frac{O_1 + O_2 + \cdots + O_n}{n} \right] \times L$
2. Average-ordinate Rule ............
$A = \frac{L}{n} [M_1 + M_2 + \cdots + O_n]$
3. Trapezoidal Rule ..................
$A = d \left[ \left(\frac{O_1 + O_{n+1}}{2}\right) O_2 + O_3 + \cdots + O_n \right]$
4. Simpson's rule ............
$A = \frac{d}{3} [(O_1 + O_{n+1}) + 4(O_2 + O_4 + \cdots) + 2(O_3 + O_5 + \cdots + O_n)]$
Select the correct answer using the codes given below:
Codes:
solution:
Trapezoidal Rule:
$A = d \left[ \left(\frac{O_1 + O_{n+1}}{2}\right) + O_2 + O_3 + \cdots + O_n \right]$
Simpson's rule:
$A = \frac{d}{3} [(O_1 + O_{n+1}) + 4(O_2 + O_4 + \cdots) + 2(O_3 + O_5 + \cdots + O_n)]$

solution:
area of circle $A = \pi r^2$
$\frac{dA}{dr} = 2\pi r$
standard error in area $e_A = e_r \cdot \frac{dA}{dr} = 0.88\text{ m}^2$

subject: Surveying
question_text: If the azimuths of the two tangents to a circular curve of radius 100 m are due north and due east, then the area bounded by the two tangents and the circular curve will be
solution:
Area of tangent square = $100 \times 100$.
Area of sector of circle = $\frac{\pi r^2}{4}$
$= \text{Area of tangent square} - \text{Area of sector of circle}$
$= 100 \times 100 - \frac{\pi 100^2}{4}$
$= 10000 - 7850$
$= 2150$ sq. m.
Hence, most approximate answer is option d.

subject: Surveying
question_text:
Consider the following statements associated with the laws of weight in the theory of errors:
1. If an equation is multiplied by its own weight, then the weight of the resulting equation is equal to the reciprocal of the weight of that equation.
2. The weight of the algebraic sum of two or more quantities is equal to the reciprocal of the sum of the individual weight.
3. If the quantity of a given weight is multiplied by a factor, then the weight of the result is obtained by dividing its given weight by the square root of that factor.
4. If the quantity of a given weight is divided by a factor, then the weight of the result is obtained by multiplying its given weight by the square of that factor.
Of these statements
solution:
It is to note that:
1. If an equation is multiplied by its own weight, then the weight of the resulting equation is equal to the reciprocal of the weight of that equation.
2. If the quantity of a given weight is divided by a factor, then the weight of the result is obtained by multiplying its given weight by the square of that factor.

subject: Surveying
question_text:
In the tangential method of tacheometry, the following notations have been used:
$\alpha_1$ = angle of elevation corresponding to upper vane
$\alpha_2$ = angle of elevation corresponding to lower vane
S = distance between the vanes - staff intercept
D = horizontal distance
V = vertical distance
In this context, match List 1st (Quantity to be computed under the given conditions) with List 2nd (Equation to be used) and select the correct answer using the codes given below the lists:
List 1st:
A. 'D' when both the angles of elevation
B. 'V' when both the angles of elevation
C. 'D' when one angle is angle of elevation and the other that of depression
D. 'V' When both angles are angles of depression
List 2nd:
1. $\frac{S\tan\alpha_2}{\tan\alpha_2 - \tan\alpha_1}$
2. $\frac{S}{\tan\alpha_2 + \tan\alpha_1}$
3. $\frac{S\tan\alpha_2}{\tan\alpha_1 - \tan\alpha_2}$
4. $\frac{S}{\tan\alpha_1 - \tan\alpha_2}$
Codes:
	A	B	C	D
solution:
'D' when both the angles of elevation = $\frac{S}{\tan\alpha_1 - \tan\alpha_2}$
'V' when both the angles of elevation = $\frac{S\tan\alpha_2}{\tan\alpha_1 - \tan\alpha_2}$
'D' when one angle is angle of elevation and the other that of depression = $\frac{S}{\tan\alpha_2 + \tan\alpha_1}$
'V' When both angles are angles of depression = $\frac{S\tan\alpha_2}{\tan\alpha_2 - \tan\alpha_1}$

question_text: A Dumpy level is set up with eyepiece vertically over a peg A. The height from the top of peg A to the centre of the eyepiece is 1.540 m and the reading on peg B is 0.705 m. The level is then set up over peg B. the height of the eyepiece above peg B is 1.490 m and the reading on A is 2.195 m. the difference in level between A and B is
solution:
A Dumpy level is set up over a peg A:
$1.540\text{ m} - 0.705\text{ m} = 0.835\text{ m}$.
When a Dumpy level is set up over a peg B:
$2.195\text{ m} - 1.490\text{ m} = 0.705\text{ m}$.
Now,
Difference in level between A and B =
$(0.835 + 0.705)/2 = 0.770\text{ m}$.

solution:
Included angle = $180^\circ - (\text{F.B of next line} - \text{B.B. of previous line})$
$= 180^\circ - (\text{F.B of BC} - \text{B.B. of AB})$
$= 180^\circ - (122^\circ - 60^\circ30')$

subject: Surveying
question_text: The tachometer focal length of object glass is 20 cm, the distance between the object glass and trunnion axis is 10 cm and the spacing between the outer lines of diaphragm axis is 4 mm. if the staff intercepts are 1.000 (top) and 2.500 (middle) when the line of collimation is perfectly horizontal, then the horizontal distance between the staff station and instrument station will be
solution:
Given:
$f = 200$mm.
$d = 100$mm.
$i = 4$mm.
$S = 2(2.5-1) = 1.5 \times 2 = 3$m.
Now, we know that horizontal distance between the staff station and instrument station is $D = KS + C$
$K = f/i = 200/4 = 50$.
$C = f + d = 200 + 100 = 300$mm.
Hence,
$D = 50 \times 3\text{m} + 0.300\text{ m} = 150.3$m.

question_text: The deflection angle between the tangents drawn at the ends of a transition curve is 7°. The radius of the curve at the end is 400 m. What is the length of the transition curve?

deflection angle = 7°
radius of the curve is 400 m
$\partial = \frac{L}{2R}$
$7 \times \frac{3.14}{180} = \frac{L}{2 \times 400}$
$L = 97.68$ m

subject: Surveying
question_text: A transition curve is required for a circular curve of 200 m radius, the gauge being 1.5 m and maximum super elevation restricted to 15 cm. the transition is to be designed for a velocity such that no lateral pressure is imposed on the rails and the rate of gain of radial acceleration is 30 cm/s³. $v^2 = \frac{0.15}{1.5} \times 9.81 \times 200$
$v = 14$ m/s
Now,
$L = \frac{14^3}{0.3 \times 200}$
$L = 46$ m

subject: Surveying
question_text: A parabolic vertical curve is set out connecting a + 0.7% grade to a -0.6% grade. The chainage and R.L. of the point of intersection are 1000 m and 250 m respectively. The admissible rate of change of grade is 0.05% per 20 m. The chainage of the tangent points will be
solution:
length of vertical curve = $\frac{G' - G''}{G} \times \text{chain length}$
length of vertical curve = $\frac{0.6 - (-0.7)}{0.05} \times 20$
Length of vertical curve = 520 m
The chainage of the tangent points = $740\text{ m} + 520\text{ m} = 1260\text{ m}$

question_text:

Of these statements

Triangulation surveying:
1. First order or Primary Triangulation
- Average triangle closure: Less than 1 second
- Maximum triangle closure: Not more than 3 seconds

- Average triangle closure: 6 sec.
- Maximum triangle closure: 12 sec.
- Length of base line: 0.5 to 3 km
- Length of sides of triangles: 1.5 to 10 km

question_text: The main plate of a transit is divided into 1080 equal division. 60 divisions of the Vernier coincide exactly with 59 divisions of the main plate. The least count (in seconds) of the transit is
solution:
Least count is equal to ratio of (length of one division on main scale to no. of divisions of Vernier) i.e. least count = $\frac{S}{n}$
$S = \frac{360}{1080}$
$n = 60$
Least count = $\frac{S}{n} = \frac{360}{60 \times 1080} = \frac{1}{180}$ degree
$= 20$ seconds

In prismoidal rule, ordinates required should be odd but here ordinates provided are even so taking average of the sides and then calculating the area. $A_1 = 24 \times 16 = 384\text{ m}^2$, $A_2 = 20 \times 12 = 240\text{ m}^2$, $A_m = (\frac{24+20}{2})(\frac{16+12}{2}) = 308\text{ m}^2$. $V = \frac{1}{3}[384 + 4 \times 308 + 240] = 618.66\text{ m}^3$

question_text:
Given that:
Scale of the photograph, 50m = 1 cm
Size of photograph = 18 cm X 18 cm
Percentage longitudinal overlap = 60
Percentage side overlap = 33 1/3
Covered area = 108 sq km
The required number of photographs will be
solution: Scale of the photograph, 50m = 1 cm. Size of photograph = 18 cm X 18 cm. Percentage longitudinal overlap = 60. Percentage side overlap = 33 1/3. Covered area = 108 sq km. Covered area in one photograph = 18X50 m X 18X50 m = 900 m X 900 m = 0.9 km X 0.9 Km. Length to covered = $\sqrt{108} = 10.39\text{ Km}$. Number of strips required lengthwise: $= \frac{L}{(1-P_L) \times l} + 1 = \frac{10.39}{(1-0.60) \times 0.9} + 1 = 29.86$. Number of strips required = $\frac{B}{(1-P_s) \times w} + 1 = \frac{10.39}{(1-0.333) \times 0.9} + 1 = 18.30$. required number of photographs = $29.86 \times 18.30 = 546 \approx 550$.

solution: Rise and Fall method is generally preferred if there are many intermediate stations in the site whereas Height of the instrument method is preferred if there are less or no intermediate stations. question_text: Assertion A: The degree of curve is directly proportional to the radius of the curve.\nReason R: A sharp curve has a large degree of curve and a flat curve has a small degree of curve.

options.a: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
options.b: Both A and R are true but R is not a correct explanation of A
options.c: A is true but R is false
options.d: A is false but R is true

solution: If R is the radius of a curve and D is degree of curve for a 30 m arc, then $R \times D \times \pi/180^\circ = 30 \Rightarrow R = \frac{30 \times 180^\circ}{D \times \pi} \Rightarrow R = \frac{1718.9}{D} \approx \frac{1719}{D}$. If D is the degree of a curve for a 20 m arc, then $R \times D \times \pi/180^\circ = 20 \Rightarrow R = \frac{20 \times 180^\circ}{D \times \pi} \Rightarrow R = \frac{1145.9}{D} \approx \frac{1146}{D}$. So, the degree of curve is inversely proportional to the radius of curve. As the degree of curve and radius of curve are inversely proportional to each other, for sharp curve (small radius curve) degree of curve is higher and vice versa. So, A is false but R is correct.
